mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz GLK 320 or 350 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM642 engine typically costs £2,500–£6,000 supply only depending on which variant (320 CDI, 350 CDI or 350 CDI BlueEFFICIENCY). Used units are available from £1,200–£2,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your GLK.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz GLK 320 / 350 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 V6 diesel is considered a 'model of reliability' and is 'very stoutly built'. However, it has well-documented weaknesses: oil cooler seal failure (a design flaw), timing chain stretch (especially on 2012–2015 models), and swirl flap motor failure. With these addressed, the engine is capable of high mileage.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz GLK 320 and 350 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil cooler seal failure (the most infamous OM642 issue — requires 9+ hours of labour), timing chain stretch (rattling on cold start), swirl flap motor failure (P2015 code, loss of low-end torque), EGR valve clogging, and turbocharger actuator issues. The oil cooler seals and timing chain are the most critical concerns.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz GLK 320/350 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: GLK values range from £5,000–£15,000 depending on condition and spec. A £2,500–£6,000 reconditioned engine is 20–50% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example with good bodywork, replacement is often worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz GLK 320 or 350 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. GLK 320 CDI (2008–2010) uses OM642.961; GLK 350 CDI (2010–2012) uses OM642.832; GLK 350 CDI BlueEFFICIENCY (2012–2015) uses OM642.835. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM642 oil cooler seals, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The oil cooler seals can be replaced — they are wear items. Replacement costs £600–£1,500 and takes approximately nine hours of labour. The seals are located deep within the 'V' of the engine, requiring removal of numerous ancillary parts. If the seals fail and cause oil starvation, the engine can be destroyed. A preventative seal replacement with updated Viton seals is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£2,500+). At the first sign of an oily mess under the engine, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz GLK 320/350 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current GLK CDI market. OM642 engines are relatively common and parts are available, but labour costs for repairs like oil cooler seals (9+ hours) can be significant. Used engines for the GLK 320 CDI (OM642.961) are more readily available than the rarer 350 CDI BlueEFFICIENCY (OM642.835).
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLK CDI engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The longitudinal V6 layout makes access reasonable, but the oil cooler location in the "V" of the engine adds complexity.

cta.label: → Compare GLK CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Oil Cooler Seal Failure (All OM642 Variants) — "The Infamous Oil Leak"
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell, visible oil residue on the engine block, low oil level warning, oily mess under the engine bay
problems.repairCost: £600–£1,500 (oil cooler seal replacement — approximately 9 hours labour)
problems.recommendation: The OM642's oil cooler seals are the most infamous weak point on this engine. The oil cooler sits within the "V" of the engine, beneath a host of ancillary parts. A "design flaw from Mercedes" means the original seals degrade over time. The updated Viton (purple) seals are an improvement, but even these can fail before 100,000 miles. Replacing two small o-rings requires approximately nine hours of labour — but it's far cheaper than a new engine. If you see an "oily mess all over the engine", book this job immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil cooler seal failure guide

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch (All OM642 Variants — Especially 2012–2015)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or slapping noise from the front of the engine, lasting 2–5 seconds after cold start, metallic ticking, engine warning light
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£4,500 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: a stretched timing chain can cause catastrophic engine failure

problems.recommendation: Timing chain stretch is a serious issue on the OM642. The chain tensioner has small notches that act as stops; when the chain stretches, the piston pushes past these stops. Owners have reported chain stretch as early as 54,600 miles. The 2008–2013 models are considered "bad years" for timing chain issues. If you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately — a preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine rebuild. The 2012–2015 BlueEFFICIENCY models appear to be particularly susceptible.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Swirl Flap Motor Failure (All OM642 Variants)
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, loss of low-end torque, hesitation on acceleration, increased emissions, irregular idle speed
problems.recommendation: The swirl flap motor on the OM642 can fail due to oil ingress through the air hose. Carbon build-up can also cause the flaps to stick. Some owners use a bypass resistor to fix the P2015 trouble code, but the proper repair is replacing the motor and cleaning the intake manifold. While the motor itself is not expensive, the labour adds up. If you see an engine warning light or experience hesitation, have the swirl flap system inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full swirl flap motor failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: GLK 320/350 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour-intensive (9+ hours) but cheaper than a new engine
Labour-intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option
Well-understood repair — bypass resistor is a cheap alternative
Cleaning or replacement — restore smooth running
Replacement or cleaning — catch before turbo damage

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLK 320/350 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Evidence of oil cooler seal replacement (preferably with updated Viton seals) — the defining OM642 issue addressed
* No rattling on cold start — timing chain should be healthy
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 10,000 miles) — the OM642 rewards good maintenance
* Post-2010 GLK 350 CDI with documented timing chain inspection
* Evidence of swirl flap motor replacement or EGR cleaning
avoidIf: * Any rattling on cold start — timing chain stretch is likely
* Oily mess under the engine — oil cooler seals are failing
* 2012–2015 BlueEFFICIENCY with no proof of timing chain work — these models are more susceptible
* Engine warning light without a clear diagnosis — swirl flap or EGR issues are expensive
* Poor service history — the OM642 is robust but unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The OM642 V6 diesel is generally a reliable engine, but it has three well-known weaknesses: oil cooler seals (a design flaw), timing chain stretch (especially on later models), and swirl flap motor failure. The oil cooler seal replacement is the most common — and the most labour-intensive — job on the OM642. With diligent maintenance and these issues addressed, the GLK CDI will reward you with smooth, effortless torque and impressive longevity. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and the OM642 will serve you well.

cta.label: → Compare GLK CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz GLK 320 or 350 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattling or slapping lasting 2–5 seconds (timing chain stretch warning — the most critical check on the OM642)
2. Oil Leaks — Any oily mess under the engine bay? (oil cooler seal failure)
3. Service History — including which generation (320 CDI, 350 CDI or 350 CDI BlueEFFICIENCY) and evidence of major maintenance
4. Oil Cooler Seal History — Has the updated Viton seal been fitted?
5. Timing Chain History — Has the chain been inspected or replaced? (especially important on 2012–2015 models)
6. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? (P2015 indicates swirl flap issues)
7. Performance — Any hesitation or loss of low-end torque? (swirl flap motor or EGR issues)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (EGR, DPF issues)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — three OM642 variants across the GLK CDI's production run.
